retrieved_docs.title: Respiratory Syncytial Virus Prefusion F (RSVPreF3) Vaccine -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: The AS01 adjuvant contained in the RSVPreF3 vaccine is a liposomal formulation that combines 2 immunostimulants that are QS-21, extracted from the bark of the Quillaja saponaria tree, and MPL, a synthetic derivative of bacterial lipopolysaccharide ( Salmonella Minnesota strain). [15] The AS01 adjuvant enhances the immune response to the RSVPreF3 antigen by activating innate immune cells, inducing cytokine production, promoting antigen presentation, and stimulating humoral and cellular immunity. [15] Moreover, the AS01 adjuvant also helps overcome the natural decline in immunity that accompanies aging by amplifying the magnitude and quality of the antibody response. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Vaccine (Vaccination) (Archived) -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Using adjuvants enhances antibody synthesis and T-cell responses. [4] Certain compounds, including aluminum salts added to immunogens, stimulate immune responses. This effect can be mediated by two essential functions: cytokine induction that regulate T and B cell functions and increased antigen presentation in sites where lymphocytes can concentrate. Many bacterial substances can activate pattern recognition receptors [5] that activate cytokine production by antigen-presenting cells.

retrieved_docs.title: Meningococcal Vaccine -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: The commercially available conjugated vaccines are usually conjugated to a protein carrier and do not contain other adjuvants in their formula. There are three available quadrivalent conjugated vaccines in the global market, all of them contain the most common polysaccharides (A, C, W, and Y), but each uses a different protein carrier: MenACWY-TT conjugated to tetanus toxoid, MenACWY-DT conjugated to diphtheria toxoid, and MenACWY-CRM conjugated to the nontoxic mutant of diphtheria toxin. Maintaining a protective and effective immune response of these licensed vaccines requires booster doses and needles for administration. Nevertheless, some limitations hinder the effectiveness and availability of these vaccines, including the high cost of the chemical conjugation, the cold chain requirements (continuous refrigeration), and the preparation of every single dose. All of the above are limitations affecting the production and distribution of conjugate vaccines. [2] [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Respiratory Syncytial Virus Prefusion F (RSVPreF3) Vaccine -- Administration
retrieved_docs.content: Preparation : Both vaccines are prepared by reconstitution. RSVPreF3 is reconstituted with the adjuvant solution, whereas RSVPreF is reconstituted with sterile water. [5] [16] Use only the diluent supplied by the manufacturer. Route : Intramuscular injection Strength : Each dose contains 120 μg of vaccine. In addition, the adjuvanted RSVPreF3 vaccine contains 25 μg of QS-21 and 25 μg of MPL. [5] Dose : A single dose of 0.5 mL is recommended for adults after reconstitution. [1] [2] Coadministration with other vaccines : Either RSVPreF3 or RSVPreF may be administered at the same visit with any other adult vaccine. [1] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Meningococcal Vaccine -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: The antigenicity of these nanoparticles is significantly higher when compared to the vaccine delivered in solution. It is also greatly potentiated when combined with some adjuvants such as alum or MF59 that become encapsulated in these nanoparticles. These alum and MF59 adjuvants have approval from the Federal and Drug Administration (FDA) for administration with licensed vaccines. There is a high potential for novel nanotechnology-based slow-release antigens to enhance antigen presentation, induce immune responses, and overcome the limitations of traditionally formulated vaccines. In addition to the low cost, its ability to be stored as a dry powder without the requirement of maintaining the cold-chain reaction facilitates storage and distribution, which in turn provides a suitable solution not only to low resource countries with endemic areas but also for vaccinations required for the annual Hajj season. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Pneumococcal Vaccine -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Both vaccines promote active immunization against the serotypes of the conjugate and capsular polysaccharides contained in the formulation of the vaccine. Immunity develops approximately 2 to 3 weeks after vaccination and lasts 5 years. However, in children and the elderly, re-immunization may be necessary sooner.

retrieved_docs.title: MMR Vaccine -- Contraindications -- Contraindications of Vaccines
retrieved_docs.content: Severe allergic reaction/anaphylaxis after a previous dose of MMR/MMRV or after one of the vaccine's components, such as neomycin and gelatin. [5] [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Typhoid Vaccine -- Mechanism of Action -- Typhoid Conjugate Vaccines
retrieved_docs.content: All TCVs contain 25 μg of Vi polysaccharide conjugated with a protein carrier. This carrier component engages B and T-cells, inducing germinal center development in the spleen and lymph nodes. Within germinal centers, polysaccharide-specific B-cells proliferate and differentiate through hypermutation of the antibody genes. Follicular helper T-cells aid this process once activated by the protein carrier presented by the major histocompatibility complex on the B-cell surface. [8] An increased affinity of the B-cell receptor antibody to the antigen increases the aid provided by T-cells via cytokines and direct cell-to-cell interaction. Repeated cycles produce higher-affinity antibodies and antibody class switching, similar to the immune response during a typical infection. B-cells in germinal centers that do not engage T-cells undergo apoptosis. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Influenza Vaccine -- Toxicity
retrieved_docs.content: The vaccine does not manifest any dose-dependent toxicity. Despite extensive studies, no toxicity regarding carcinogenicity and infertility has been found. The components of the influenza vaccine are the following: Formaldehyde which is used to inactivate toxins from viruses and bacteria. Thimerosal which safeguards against contamination and is only present in multi-dose vials. Aluminum salts which act as adjuvants and impart a more robust immune response. Gelatin which is present as a stabilizer. Antibiotics, such as gentamicin or neomycin, which are present in the flu vaccine to keep bacteria from growing. Toxicity does not exist due to the inconspicuous amounts of these components in the vaccine. [17] [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Hepatitis A Vaccine -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Many vaccines contain substances called adjuvants that help to increase the immune system's response to a vaccine. HAV contains aluminum, which some think delays the absorption of the injected antigens, resulting in a high concentration of antigens at the local injection site. Aluminum may also directly activate cells of the innate immune system, causing the release of inflammatory mediators and thus enhancing the immune response as described above. [1] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Allergy Immunotherapy -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes -- Current and Future Trends in Allergen Immunotherapy
retrieved_docs.content: Recent advances in understanding the mechanism and the long-term effects of immunotherapy are encouraging for future therapies. There are also new approaches being used to improve safety and overcoming the risk of severe adverse allergic reactions during immunotherapy. Newer allergen preparations available include allergoids, recombinant allergens (recA), and modified-recombinant allergens (recA). Studies on virus-like particles and CpG-motifs, adjuvants like MPL, and aluminum hydroxide have been shown to increase immunological response and can improve safety and efficacy. [8] Other newer approaches to allergen immunotherapy include the application of extract patches on the skin and/or inguinal lymph node injection. Furthermore, recombinant technology or chemicals may alter allergen molecules that make them less reactive; this may be due to suppression of Th2 responses or stimulation of toll-like receptors (approval is pending). [28] The new advances in allergy immunotherapy not only provide disease-modifying treatments but are also cost-effective and improve the quality of life. [29] Yet, with the historical efficacy and safety of immunotherapy, it remains underutilized.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Lipopolysaccharide -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Enhance the effect of Vaccines and Drugs: TLR activation is inducible by low levels of endotoxin, present in vaccines and other immunotherapeutics, which provides improved immunization and drug effect, leading to better therapeutic outcomes. Low levels of LPS present in adjuvant can enhance their activity. Hence, the term for it is innate immune response modulation impurities (IIRMIs). [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Influenza Vaccine -- Adverse Effects -- Administration with Other Vaccines
retrieved_docs.content: IIV4 and RIV4 may be administered concurrently/sequentially with other inactivated/live vaccines. However, injectable vaccines given at the same time should be administered at separate anatomic sites. Clinicians should refer to the latest CDC/ACIP recommendations/guidance for COVID-19 vaccines and administering influenza vaccines. The LAIV4 vaccine may be administered simultaneously with other live or inactivated vaccines. If not given simultaneously, administer 4 weeks or more apart between the administration of LAIV4 and another live vaccine. The safety and immunogenicity of simultaneous or sequential administration of 2 vaccines containing non-aluminum adjuvants have not yet been studied.

retrieved_docs.title: Vaccine (Vaccination) (Archived) -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Live vaccines are most effective than killed vaccines because they retain more antigens of the microbes. However, toxoids, including those that cause tetanus and diphtheria, are the most effective bacterial vaccines because their effect is based on inactivated exotoxins that stimulate strong antibody production. Subunit vaccines, including hepatitis B, meningococcal, and Hemophilus influenzae B vaccines, are effective when conjugated to carrier proteins such as tetanus toxoid. Vaccinologists produce subunit vaccines either by recombinant DNA technology or by antigen purification from different bacterial strains. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Aluminum Hydroxide -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: As an adjuvant in vaccines, aluminum hydroxide is known to increase macrophage phagocytosis, possibly via the upregulation of the NLRP3-inflammasome, increasing uptake of the desired antigen. Additionally, it is known to facilitate what is known as the "repository effect," whereby the antigens aggregate on and around the molecule, which helps to prevent their degradation within the body. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 4 -- Community 2828
retrieved_docs.content: Immunological adjuvancia (immunological adjuvant) refers to agents that are employed to boost immune responses, and they can be formulated in diverse ways. One formulation category includes drug combinations that contain amylase, phosphates, and proteases, which together act as an immunological adjuvant by providing enzymatic and mineral components that modulate antigen presentation and immune activation. Another type of immunological adjuvant involves the steroid precursor dehydroepiandrosteron (dehydroepiandrosterone), which can function as an immunomodulatory substance enhancing immune function. Thus, both amylase‑phosphate‑protease drug combinations and dehydroepiandrosteron are recognized as specific instances of immunological adjuvants, illustrating the breadth of compounds that can serve this supportive role in immunotherapy and vaccine strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1531
retrieved_docs.content: Viral antigens constitute the active ingredient of viral vaccines, providing the essential immunogenic component that stimulates the body’s immune response and confers protection against the targeted viral infections.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1385
retrieved_docs.content: Toxicological considerations also extend to the development, evaluation, and administration of viral vaccines, requiring vigilance for any toxic risks associated with vaccine components or adjuvants. Comprehensive awareness of the toxic profiles of these agents, together with systematic application of the Allowed qualifier, is essential for optimizing patient care and ensuring the safety of therapeutic and preventive interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1295
retrieved_docs.content: Inosin pranobex and dehydroepiandrosterone are both classified as immunological adjuvants, meaning they function as agents that enhance or modulate the body’s immune response. As immunological adjuvants, these substances are employed to boost immunity in various therapeutic contexts, such as improving vaccine efficacy or supporting immune function in patients with compromised immune systems. Their shared categorization underscores the role of diverse chemical compounds in augmenting immune activity and highlights their relevance in clinical strategies aimed at strengthening host defenses.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 1217
retrieved_docs.content: Immunology and metabolism are tightly linked through a diverse set of molecules, pathogens, clinical conditions and therapeutic agents. The bacterium Corynebacterium diphtheriae produces a toxin that elicits protective antibodies after vaccination and is associated with other upper‑respiratory infections. Hormones that regulate metabolism—adrenocorticotropic hormone (ACTH), glucagon and erythropoietin—also modulate immune activity, while enzymes and proteins such as amylases, blood‑coagulation factors, bone morphogenetic proteins, and the bile acid chenodeoxycholic acid can serve as antigens, influence immune pathways, or reflect metabolic status. Common allergens, the anticholinergic drug atropine, cardiac glycosides (including the cardiotonic strophanthin), and mydriatic agents (atropine and cyclopentolate) interact with both immune and metabolic processes. Herbal preparations such as silymarin, head fern (سعفة الرأس) and lemongrass are detectable in urine and may affect immune

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2100
retrieved_docs.content: Injection or infusion of therapeutic or prophylactic substances and prophylactic vaccination or inoculation against certain bacterial diseases are both classified under the broader category of Therapeutic Interventions: Immunization, Injections, Perfusion. This parent category encompasses medical procedures that deliver therapeutic agents directly into the body, either through injection or infusion, as well as preventive measures such as bacterial vaccinations. By grouping these specific interventions within the same therapeutic framework, clinicians recognize that both the administration of therapeutic or prophylactic substances and the use of bacterial vaccines are integral components of immunization and perfusion strategies aimed at preventing disease and delivering treatment effectively.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 2 -- Community 441
retrieved_docs.content: in managing constipation; toxicity evaluation is explicitly required for infant‑formula substitutes, conventional vaccines and viral vaccines. Recognition of these dual therapeutic‑toxic roles, classification hierarchies, enzymatic inhibition mechanisms and contraindications supports comprehensive patient assessment, safety monitoring and optimization of pharmacotherapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 4 -- Community 2740
retrieved_docs.content: Hormony is described as a broad category of agents that influence physiological processes, and medications and vaccines are classified as a subset within this category, reflecting their role in modulating bodily functions in a manner analogous to hormones.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 88
retrieved_docs.content: and urological activity, providing relief of urinary‑tract discomfort. The vaccine Typhim VI contains the inactive ingredient fenol. Together, these entities and relationships provide a comprehensive framework for understanding how application, dosage, qualifying agents, physiological context, safety considerations and formulation choices intersect in patient‑centered pharmacotherapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 1759
retrieved_docs.content: Prophylactic vaccination or inoculation against certain bacterial diseases is classified as a therapeutic intervention and is a subcategory of the broader group termed Therapeutic Interventions: Immunization, Injections, Perfusion. This relationship indicates that preventive vaccination against bacterial pathogens is considered part of the overall therapeutic strategies that include various forms of immunization, injectable treatments, and perfusion techniques, highlighting its role within comprehensive patient care aimed at preventing infection.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 527
retrieved_docs.content: The term “vakcíny” (vaccines) serves as an overarching category that includes several sub‑classes and individual products. One sub‑class is “bacterial and viral vaccines, combined,” which is recognized as a type of vaccine and comprises formulations such as the DTPP vaccine, which is a member of this combined bacterial‑viral group. Another sub‑class under vakcíny is “toxoidy,” indicating vaccines that are based on toxoids. Specific vaccine products that fall within the vakcíny hierarchy include the “typhoid Vi polysaccharide vaccine, S typhi Ty2 strain 0.05 MG/ML Injectable Solution [Typhim VI],” which is explicitly classified as a vaccine and contains phenol as an inactive ingredient. The “SNC‑rCTB vaccine” is also mapped to the vakcíny category, confirming its status as a vaccine. Together, these relationships illustrate how vakcíny encompasses combined bacterial‑viral vaccines, toxoid‑based vaccines, and individual licensed products such as Typhim VI, SNC‑rCTB, and DTPP, each with distinct compositions and classifications relevant to vaccine development and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 1492
retrieved_docs.content: Adjuvancia imunologická functions as a category of immunological adjuvants that includes both inosin pranobex and dehydroepiandrosteron. Inosin pranobex is therefore recognized as an immunological adjuvant, while dehydroepiandrosteron likewise serves as an immunological adjuvant and is additionally classified within the ATC group “other sex hormones and modulators of the genital system.” This dual classification of dehydroepiandrosteron highlights its role not only in enhancing immune responses but also as a hormone‑related agent that modulates genital system function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 1944
retrieved_docs.content: Medications and vaccines are categorized as neurological agents, indicating that this therapeutic group belongs to the broader class of agents that act on the nervous system.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1392
retrieved_docs.content: Adjuvancia imunologická, an immunological adjuvant, includes several specific agents. The formulation described as “amylase, phosphates, proteases drug combinations” is classified as a type of immunological adjuvant and is mapped to amylasy, indicating a direct relationship with the amylase enzyme. In addition, the antiviral‑immune modulator inosin pranobex and the steroid precursor dehydroepiandrosteron are also regarded as subtypes of adjuvancia imunologická. Recognizing these substances as members of the broader immunological adjuvant category clarifies their mechanistic connections and potential synergistic roles in enhancing immune responses.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 1 -- Community 165
retrieved_docs.content: Alantoin is classified as a dermatological substance, which places it within the broader category of medications and vaccines. As a dermatological agent, alantoin is also listed as an inactive ingredient of the capsaicin 0.75 mg/mL topical cream. Dermatological substances themselves are organized hierarchically: they include keratolytics and furocoumarins as child subclasses, and they are considered a type of medications and vaccines. Vaccines (vakcíny) are a subclass of medications and vaccines and encompass several more specific groups. Bacterial and viral combined vaccines are a child category of vaccines, and the DTPP vaccine is a member of this combined‑vaccine group. Toxoids are another child subclass of vaccines. Specific vaccine products such as the typhoid Vi polysaccharide vaccine (Typhim VI) and the SNC‑rCTB vaccine are mapped to the vaccine category; the Typhim VI preparation contains phenol as an inactive ingredient. Hypolipidemics are also defined as a subclass of medications and vaccines. Within this group, butoxamin and diallyl trisulfide are identified as hypolipidemic agents, and ispaghula husk is listed as a child subclass of hypolipidemics. Together, these classifications illustrate how individual agents such as alantoin, phenol, butoxamin, diallyl trisulfide, and ispaghula husk fit into larger therapeutic families, and how products like capsaicin cream, combined bacterial‑viral vaccines, toxoid vaccines, and specific vaccines (Typhim VI, SNC‑rCTB) are organized within the overarching framework of medications and vaccines. This integrated understanding supports accurate prescribing, formulation design, and patient‑centered care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 409
retrieved_docs.content: Vaccines (vakcíny) represent a broad class of immunizations that include several specialized subcategories. Combined bacterial and viral vaccines, referred to as “bacterial and viral vaccines, combined,” are a distinct group within the overall vaccine category, and the DTPP vaccine is an example that belongs to this combined bacterial‑viral group. Toxoidy (toxoid vaccines) is a child subcategory of vaccines, focusing on inactivated toxins as immunogens. Viral vaccines constitute another subclass of vaccines; the active ingredients of these formulations are viral antigens (antigeny virové). The SNC‑rCTB vaccine is mapped to the general vaccine class, indicating that it is also a vaccine. Together, these relationships illustrate how various vaccine types—combined bacterial/viral formulations, toxoid preparations, and viral antigen‑based products—are organized under the overarching concept of vaccines, highlighting their shared purpose of preventing infectious diseases and clarifying how specific products such as DTPP and SNC‑rCTB fit within this taxonomy.
